title: ShadowCache: A Page-Cache State-Ablation Study of How Much Linux-Observable State Policy Ranking Needs
description: ShadowCache is a Linux page-cache-specific ablation study rather than a new replay framework. It asks how much Linux-observable state is actually needed to preserve the ranking of a fixed six-policy page-cache pool, using an extended replay reference that is itself anchored by one mandatory six-policy live check per workload family.
motivation: PageFlex and cache_ext make programmable Linux page-cache policies practical, but they do not answer the narrower evaluation question of which observable state variables are necessary for policy ranking. Prior cache-evaluation work such as Miniature Simulations, SHARDS, AET, and Kosmo studies cheap approximate cache modeling in more general settings, leaving open whether ranking among deployable Linux page-cache policies can be preserved with much less replay state than a rich page-cache trace.
proposed_approach: Collect a richer Linux page-cache trace with exact logical page accesses plus only defensible kernel-visible annotations: insertions, evictions, dirty or writeback state, and coarse reclaim epochs. Derive a four-level trace hierarchy (`ExtendedHinted`, `CompactState`, `NoDirty`, `AccessOnly`), replay a fixed six-policy pool (`LinuxDefault`, `FIFO`, `CLOCK`, `LFU`, `S3FIFO`, `Hyperbolic`), and measure ranking degradation as each state family is removed. Validate the credibility of the richer replay reference with a reference-stability ablation and one mandatory six-policy live anchor for each of three workload families: streaming scan, SQLite reuse, and realistic mixed file service.

related_work.title: PageFlex: Flexible and Efficient User-space Delegation of Linux Paging Policies with eBPF
related_work.authors: Anil Yelam, Kan Wu, Zhiyuan Guo, Suli Yang, Rajath Shashidhara, Wei Xu, Stanko Novakovic, Alex C. Snoeren, Kimberly Keeton
related_work.how_it_differs: PageFlex already demonstrates Linux paging-policy delegation, trace collection, and replay. ShadowCache asks the narrower follow-on question of which Linux-observable state families are actually needed for policy ranking.

related_work.title: cache_ext: Customizing the Page Cache with eBPF
related_work.authors: Tal Zussman, Ioannis Zarkadas, Jeremy Carin, Andrew Cheng, Hubertus Franke, Jonas Pfefferle, Asaf Cidon
related_work.how_it_differs: cache_ext provides a programmable page-cache substrate. ShadowCache is an evaluation study above that substrate, focused on ranking-preserving state ablations.

related_work.title: StreamCache: Revisiting Page Cache for File Scanning on Fast Storage Devices
related_work.how_it_differs: StreamCache studies a specialized page-cache design for file scanning. ShadowCache instead studies which observable state is needed to rank multiple policies across scan, database, and mixed-service workloads.

related_work.title: Cache Modeling and Optimization using Miniature Simulations
related_work.authors: Carl A. Waldspurger, Trausti Saemundsson, Irfan Ahmad, Nohhyun Park
related_work.how_it_differs: Miniature Simulations studies low-cost simulation for general cache modeling and optimization, but not Linux page-cache-specific state ablations for policy ordering.

related_work.title: Efficient MRC Construction with SHARDS
related_work.authors: Carl A. Waldspurger, Nohhyun Park, Alexander Garthwaite, Irfan Ahmad
related_work.how_it_differs: SHARDS makes miss-ratio-curve construction cheaper through sampling, but it does not ask how much Linux-visible page-cache state is necessary to preserve a fixed policy ranking.

related_work.title: Kinetic Modeling of Data Eviction in Cache
related_work.authors: Xiameng Hu, Xiaolin Wang, Lan Zhou, Yingwei Luo, Chen Ding, Zhenlin Wang
related_work.how_it_differs: AET targets lightweight cache modeling for LRU-like settings; ShadowCache targets ranking preservation across six Linux page-cache policies with live anchoring.

related_work.title: Kosmo: Efficient Online Miss Ratio Curve Generation for Eviction Policy Evaluation
related_work.authors: Kia Shakiba, Sari Sultan, Michael Stumm
related_work.how_it_differs: Kosmo evaluates eviction policies from shared traces online, but it does not analyze Linux page-cache state ablations or validate a richer replay reference against live full-pool runs.

related_work.title: Modeling the Linux page cache for accurate simulation of data-intensive applications
related_work.authors: Hoang-Dung Do, Valerie Hayot-Sasson, Rafael Ferreira da Silva, Christopher Steele, Henri Casanova, Tristan Glatard
related_work.how_it_differs: This work asks how to simulate Linux page cache accurately. ShadowCache asks how much Linux-observable state can be removed while retaining ranking signal.

hypothesis: A compact Linux page-cache trace that keeps logical page accesses plus dirty or writeback state and coarse reclaim epochs will preserve full-pool policy ranking much better than an access-only trace, and it will do so consistently enough across three workload families that the retained ranking signal can be validated against a richer replay reference and three live full-pool anchors.

confirm: CompactState reaches mean top-2 set recall of at least 0.80 and mean Kendall tau of at least 0.65 versus ExtendedHinted over the six-policy pool.
AccessOnly is at least 0.10 worse than CompactState on mean Kendall tau or mean top-2 set recall, demonstrating that some Linux-observable state is necessary.
The alternate richer reference without explicit insert or evict hints still achieves mean top-2 set recall of at least 0.80 versus ExtendedHinted.
On the three mandatory six-policy live anchors, ExtendedHinted and CompactState each recover the live top-2 set in at least two cases.
The complete study remains within the 2-core, CPU-only, 8-hour budget.
refute: CompactState offers little or no ranking benefit over AccessOnly.
Nearly all extended-trace fields are required, leaving no useful compression frontier.
The two richer replay references disagree too often to treat the extended trace as a credible ablation target.
Live full-pool anchors show that both richer and compact replay are poor proxies for actual policy ranking.
Runtime or tracing cost breaks the stated hardware and time budget.
